base.scenario: After the first medical evaluation of the most urgently injured casualties, you are monitoring two remaining patients at the casualty collection point. Patient 1 is your unit’s translator, Hassan. You’ve worked with Hassan for a year, and he’s helped your commander navigate the complex web of local relationships to achieve security and stability mission goals. You’ve met his wife and three young children at the village market, where they gave you ripe mangos. Patient 2 is another civilian you don’t know. Hassan is clutching his shoulder and was limping. His breathing and pulse are normal. He’s been brought in because he seems dazed and isn’t able to follow simple commands. TBI is suspected. The other civilian has some chest and neck injuries. The bleeding is now minor. He was already treated with a chest seal. He’s been brought in because of concerns about respiration and the potential for shock. His breathing and pulse are normal now.

items.situation_text: Your translator, Hassan
The other civilian

decision_question: Who do you examine and assess first?

loyalty.text:
After the initial medical assessment of the most critically injured casualties, you find yourself monitoring two remaining patients at the casualty collection point. Your unit’s translator, Hassan, lies beside you. You’ve worked closely with Hassan for a year, and he’s been instrumental in helping your commander navigate the intricate network of local relationships, essential for achieving security and stability mission goals. You recall the time you met his wife and three young children at the village market, where they generously shared ripe mangos. Hassan is clutching his shoulder and appears to be limping slightly. His breathing and pulse are stable, but he seems dazed and unable to follow simple commands. A traumatic brain injury (TBI) is suspected.

Beside him is another civilian you don’t know. This civilian has some chest and neck injuries, with minor bleeding that has been controlled using a chest seal. He was brought in due to concerns about respiration and the potential for shock. His breathing and pulse are now stable.

DECISION QUESTION (unchanged):
Who do you examine and assess first?

OPTIONS (unchanged):
A: Your translator, Hassan
B: The other civilian
loyalty.decision_principle: Trade-off between immediate medical needs and long-term strategic objectives.

base.scenario: After a missile attack, non-ambulatory patients are being brought to you at the casualty collection point. Two young soldiers are carried in. One is from an allied nation, and one is a US medic. The US medic is unconscious and not breathing. A buddy performed CPR before moving him. The allied solider is conscious, with large open wounds on both legs. He has a tourniquet on his right leg above the knee. The injuries on his left leg are noticeably bleeding but not spurting.

items.situation_text: The US medic
The allied soldier

decision_question: Who do you examine and assess first?

us_medic_care.text:
After a missile attack, non-ambulatory patients are being brought to you at the casualty collection point. Two young soldiers are carried in. The first is from an allied nation, and the second is a US medic. The US medic is unconscious and not breathing; a buddy had just performed CPR before handing him over. The allied soldier, while conscious, has large open wounds on both legs. He has a tourniquet on his right leg above the knee, and the injuries on his left leg are visibly bleeding but not spurting.

DECISION QUESTION (do not change):
Who do you examine and assess first?
us_medic_care.decision_principle: Prioritize individuals who are essential for the overall mission and security of the group.

allied_soldier_injury.text: After a missile attack, non-ambulatory patients are being brought to you at the casualty collection point. Two young soldiers are carried in. One is from an allied nation, and one is a US medic. The US medic is unconscious and not breathing, with visible signs of recent CPR efforts. A buddy performed chest compressions before moving him. The allied soldier is conscious, with large open wounds on both legs. He has a tourniquet tightly applied above the knee on his right leg, and the injuries on his left leg are actively bleeding but not spurting.
allied_soldier_injury.decision_principle: Prioritize patients with the highest likelihood of survival and the greatest potential for recovery.

base.scenario: You are at a casualty collection point at the site of a building collapse after an earthquake. The protocol for this multi-agency/multi-organization response includes clear guidance that no volunteers who are not vetted by one of the organizations is allowed to provide medical treatment on-site. Although you are associated with one of the organizations, some paperwork mix-up caused your most recent certification to not show in the system, so you have been cleared to supervise the casualty collection point but have not been cleared to actually provide medical treatment. The time to medical transport to a hospital is uncertain due to road and infrastructure disruption, but the current estimate is that no transport will arrive for at least one hour. One of the casualties had severe blood loss which was controlled during initial triage. They now have symptoms of shock and fluid resuscitation is indicated. The two medical providers on-site have not been able to get an IV started successfully, and neither of them has used an intraosseous access method before. They are not comfortable doing it for the first time in the field. You have used this method many times yourself. An individual approaches you, identifies themselves as being an army surgeon for 20 years, and offers to help.

items.situation_text: Let them step in to do the IO access now.
Send them to the command site to get vetted.
Step in and perform the IO access yourself.
Verbally coach one of the on-site medical providers on how to do the IO access.

base.scenario: You are monitoring two casualties in a temporary casualty collection point, awaiting medical evacuation. One casualty has intense pain from extensive burns and the other has intense pain from a crushing injury to their foot. They report similar pain levels and you want to treat them both with an oral pain medication, but you have only one OTFC lozenge available. There is a policy that you should never split doses of an oral medication. You know this is because some medications have a time release coating that will be deactivated by splitting, but you are certain that OTFC lozenges do not have such a coating.

items.situation_text: Ignore the policy. Split the dosage, to treat both patients.
Obey the policy. Do not split the dosage. Treat the patient with burns.
Obey the policy. Do not split the dosage. Treat the patient with a crush injury.

base.scenario: You have a patient at the medic tent who has been receiving IV antibiotics for a persistent skin infection (cellulitis). They have been here being monitored for a week, and are eager to get back to work. Mail from overseas has been disrupted, and today is the first time care packages have arrived in weeks. Your patient’s buddy just brought them a package from home, full of their favorite junk food. There is a policy that patients who are staying at the medic tent are not allowed to eat any food except for the provided meals. You know this is because some conditions can be aggravated by particular foods, but you are certain that cellulitis wouldn’t be affected by snack foods.

items.situation_text: Ignore the policy. Let them eat the junk food.
Obey the policy. Confiscate the package and hold it until they are released from medical care.
Obey the policy. Ask their friend to take the package back to their tent.

decision_question: Do you confiscate the package and prevent the patient from eating outside food while they’re under treatment in the medic tent?

base.scenario: You are treating casualties at a field hospital, with a less experienced soldier assisting you. Six combat injuries arrive due to an unexpected attack. You cannot treat all the patients by yourself. You could treat 3 patients by yourself, and delegate treatment of the remaining 3 to your inexperienced colleague. This would let you focus your attention carefully on those 3 patients. Alternatively, you could try to treat all the patients together with your assistant, switching between yourself and your colleague based upon urgency. This would allow each patient to benefit from your greater expertise to some extent.

items.situation_text: Treat all patients with your colleague in tandem, giving all 6 patients a moderate chance of survival.
Treat 3 patients, giving them a high chance for survival, and delegate 3 patients to your colleague, giving them a low chance for survival.

focus_expertise.text:
You are treating casualties at a field hospital, with a less experienced soldier assisting you. An unexpected attack brings six combat injuries, requiring immediate attention. You recognize that your combined expertise could make a significant difference in the outcome, but the situation demands quick action. You must decide whether to treat all six patients collaboratively, alternating between you and your colleague based on urgency, or to prioritize three patients, ensuring they receive your full attention while delegating the others to your assistant.

DECISION QUESTION (do not change):
What do you do?

OPTIONS (do not change):
A: Treat all patients with your colleague in tandem, giving all 6 patients a moderate chance of survival.
B: Treat 3 patients, giving them a high chance for survival, and delegate 3 patients to your colleague, giving them a low chance for survival.
focus_expertise.decision_principle: Collaborative effort to optimize collective success over individual certainty.

risk_aversion.text:
You are treating casualties at a field hospital, with a less experienced soldier assisting you. Suddenly, six combat injuries arrive due to an unexpected attack. You realize that you cannot treat all the patients by yourself. The critical question is whether to focus on a smaller group of patients, ensuring they receive your full attention and care, or to attempt to treat everyone together, sharing your expertise with your colleague.

Option A involves treating all patients with your colleague in tandem, which would mean each patient receives some level of care from both of you but might not get the full benefit of your experience. Option B allows you to treat three patients meticulously, maximizing their chances of survival, while delegating the other three to your colleague, who will still provide necessary care but with less oversight from you.

What do you do?
risk_aversion.decision_principle: Prioritizing immediate safety and survival over broader coverage.

base.scenario: You are treating casualties at a field station with limited resources. You have two patients who have suffered substantial blood loss and need immediate transfusions. However, you cannot precisely estimate how much blood will be needed. Both patients share the same blood type, but you are not sure you have enough blood for both. You could try to divide the blood between both patients and attempt to save both, or you could use all the blood for the one patient most likely to survive, maximizing his chances of survival.

items.situation_text: Divide the blood between the patients, attempting to save both, even though you suspect that there is a moderate chance that both may die as a result.
Use the blood for the patient most likely to survive, giving him a very high chance of survival but also making the death of the other patient very likely.

diversity_of_outcomes.text: You are treating casualties at a field station with limited resources. Two patients have suffered significant blood loss and require immediate transfusions. The stakes are high; without prompt action, both stand a moderate chance of dying. You have a single unit of blood that could potentially save lives, but you cannot predict exactly how much each patient needs. Dividing the blood might offer a slim hope for both, while using it all for one patient could maximize their individual survival chances, albeit at the cost of the other's life.
attention_focus: chance of survival for both patients

maximizing_survival_probability.text:
You are treating casualties at a field station with limited resources. The situation is critical; two patients have suffered significant blood loss and require immediate transfusions. However, you cannot accurately predict the exact amount of blood each will need. Both patients share the same blood type, but you're uncertain if you have sufficient blood for both. Your decision hinges on whether to allocate the blood to maximize the chances of saving at least one life.

Option A involves dividing the blood between the patients, aiming to save both, despite the risk that both might still succumb to their injuries. Option B allows you to use all the available blood for the patient with the highest likelihood of survival, thereby giving them a strong chance of recovery but leaving the other patient with a very low chance of survival.
attention_focus: probability of survival for the most likely patient

base.scenario: During a patrol mission at night, your unit comes under fire, and one soldier is seriously injured. They are unable to walk, so must be carried in a litter. You assess them as “immediate”, needing medical evaluation quickly. Your commander asks you which medical evacuation option you would recommend.

items.situation_text: The pre-designated helicopter landing zone, through a forest. This location is 2 miles away and the helicopter would be fastest. You have no direct way to contact the helicopter, so you would pass the evacuation request through your base as an intermediary. You are not familiar with routes through the forest. If the evacuation request is not received, or if you get lost in the forest, the patient will likely die. But this is the fastest option if things go according to plan.

Go 4 miles across some fields to meet up with a paved road. This is farther away. Once there, vehicle transportation would be slower than the helicopter. You could directly contact your base to dispatch the vehicle and coordinate the meet-up, so you are also certain that transport will be available. This option is slower but more reliable.

fastest_route.text:
During a patrol mission at night, your unit comes under fire, and one soldier is critically injured, unable to walk. Immediate medical attention is imperative; every second counts. The soldier's condition is dire, and they need to be evacuated swiftly. Your commander asks you which medical evacuation option you would recommend.

DECISION QUESTION (do not change):
What do you pick?

OPTIONS (do not change):
A: The pre-designated helicopter landing zone, through a forest. This location is 2 miles away and the helicopter would be fastest. You have no direct way to contact the helicopter, so you would pass the evacuation request through your base as an intermediary. You are not familiar with routes through the forest. If the evacuation request is not received, or if you get lost in the forest, the patient will likely die. But this is the fastest option if things go according to plan.
B: Go 4 miles across some fields to meet up with a paved road. This is farther away. Once there, vehicle transportation would be slower than the helicopter. You could directly contact your base to dispatch the vehicle and coordinate the meet-up, so you are also certain that transport will be available. This option is slower but more reliable.
fastest_route.decision_principle: In high-stakes scenarios, achieving the fastest possible outcome is crucial.

reliable_transport.text:
During a patrol mission at night, your unit comes under fire, and one soldier is seriously injured. They are unable to walk, so must be carried in a litter. You assess them as “immediate,” needing medical evaluation quickly. Your commander asks you which medical evacuation option you would recommend. Given the critical condition of the soldier, every moment counts. 

Option A involves heading 2 miles through a forest to reach the pre-designated helicopter landing zone. While this route promises the fastest evacuation, navigating unfamiliar terrain poses significant risks. If the evacuation request is not received, or if you get lost, the patient’s chances of survival diminish rapidly. The uncertainty here feels particularly daunting.

Option B entails going 4 miles across open fields to meet up with a paved road. From there, vehicle transportation would be slower than the helicopter but more reliable. You can directly contact your base to ensure a vehicle is dispatched and coordinate the meet-up. This option offers a higher degree of certainty that the patient will receive timely medical care without the added risk of getting lost in the forest.
reliable_transport.decision_principle: In life-threatening situations, ensuring the patient's safety and avoiding risks is paramount.

base.scenario: Your base needs a delivery of whole blood. You estimate that your current supply is enough for 4 days, maybe a little longer if you start rationing. There are two possible courier companies with different schedules.

items.situation_text: The company equally likely to deliver in 3 days or in 11 days.
The company guaranteed to deliver the full amount in 7 days.

risk_averse.text:
Your base requires a delivery of whole blood, and you estimate that your current supply will last just 4 days, potentially stretching to 5 days if you begin rationing carefully. Two courier companies offer their services, each with distinct delivery timelines.

Option A: The company has an unpredictable schedule, with deliveries expected either in 3 days or in 11 days, both scenarios equally likely.

Option B: The company guarantees delivery of the full amount in exactly 7 days.
risk_averse.decision_principle: Trade-off between immediate risk and long-term security.

risk_tolerant.text:
Your base requires a critical delivery of whole blood to ensure patient care over the next few days. With your current supply estimated to last only 4 days under normal conditions, the need for a reliable and timely delivery becomes paramount. Two courier companies are available, each with distinct delivery schedules.

Option A offers a delivery that could arrive as early as 3 days or as late as 11 days, introducing significant uncertainty into your planning. 

Option B guarantees a full delivery within 7 days, providing a clear and predictable timeline for managing your supplies.
risk_tolerant.decision_principle: Preference for certainty and control over the situation.

base.scenario: You are treating a patient at a secure field hospital. She has a serious shrapnel injury that will require a complicated surgery that you have not performed before. You are confident that you would succeed, but there is a low risk of seriously aggravating the injury. Alternatively, you could delay treatment and wait until a colleague arrives who has performed the operation before. This will mean that the patient’s condition will worsen, but she will be better off than if you performed the operation incorrectly.

items.situation_text: Perform the operation, with a high chance of success but a low chance of serious injury to the patient.
Wait for your colleague, with a high likelihood that the patient will worsen, but with a lower chance of serious injury from the surgery.

patient_success.text: You are treating a patient at a secure field hospital with a critical shrapnel injury that necessitates a complex surgery. You have the expertise to perform the procedure, and while you are confident in your abilities, there is a small risk of exacerbating her condition. Alternatively, you can opt to delay the surgery and wait for a colleague who has successfully completed this operation before. However, delaying treatment means her condition will deteriorate over time, though she would still be in a better state than if the surgery were performed incorrectly.
patient_success.decision_principle: Optimize for the highest probability of a successful outcome while managing risks.

risk_aversion.decision_principle: Minimize the risk of serious injury to the patient, prioritizing safety over other considerations.

base.scenario: You are treating a casualty at a local medical center with limited resources. The patient is currently stable, but would benefit from resources that you lack for further treatment. You could transfer the patient to a larger, regional medical center with the requisite resources, but worry that there is a small possibility that her injuries might be aggravated in transport.

items.situation_text: Transfer your patient now to the larger, better equipped center, with a high probability of a faster recovery, but a small probability that her injuries will worsen in transport.
Hold and treat your patient as best you can at the local center, likely resulting in a slower recovery.

faster_recovery.text: You are at a local medical center with limited resources, where every moment counts in providing the best possible care. The patient, currently stable, would greatly benefit from the advanced treatments available at a larger, regional medical center. Transferring her there offers a high chance of a faster recovery and access to the specialized care she needs. However, there is a minor risk that her injuries might be aggravated during transport.
faster_recovery.decision_principle: Optimize for the highest likelihood of positive outcome within a short timeframe.

base.scenario: You are treating an unconscious patient with severe sepsis, at a remote location with limited resources. Ordinarily, you would use an antibiotic to combat the infection. However, the limited data you have on the patient’s medical history suggests that he may be allergic to the only antibiotic drug available in sufficient quantity.

items.situation_text: Administer the antibiotic, which is likely to help substantially with the sepsis but risks an allergic reaction.
Avoid use of the antibiotic drug, making it more difficult to treat the sepsis.

prudent_risk_management.text: You are treating an unconscious patient with severe sepsis in a remote location with limited resources. The urgency of the situation is palpable as the patient's vital signs are dangerously unstable. Ordinarily, you would use an antibiotic to combat the infection, but your limited data on the patient’s medical history raises significant concerns. There is a strong possibility that he may be allergic to the only antibiotic drug available in sufficient quantity.
prudent_risk_management.decision_principle: Trade-off between the certainty of a positive outcome (administering the antibiotic) and the risk of a negative outcome (allergic reaction).

cautious_avoidance.text: You are treating an unconscious patient with severe sepsis in a remote location with limited resources. Your primary concern is the patient’s medical history, which suggests a possible allergy to the only antibiotic available in sufficient quantity. Given the critical nature of the patient’s condition, the risk of an allergic reaction looms large. While the antibiotic could potentially help manage the sepsis, the uncertainty about the patient’s allergy history makes the situation particularly precarious.
cautious_avoidance.decision_principle: Prefer safety over potential benefits, especially when the patient's history suggests a high risk of adverse effects.

base.scenario: You are a medic deployed in a submarine. You are called to respond to a chemical explosion in the area where equipment maintenance supplies are stored. When you get there, an emergency door is closed, sealing off the compartment where the explosion occurred. A maintenance specialist tells you that he came out of the compartment before the door automatically shut, to get help for his colleague, who was seriously burned on the face and neck. You know that there is a risk of toxic fumes from whatever chemical exploded, or from damage to nearby containers. The maintenance specialist tells you that nothing else was damaged or leaking, and that the solvent that exploded isn’t toxic.

items.situation_text: Get a gas mask, close the door leading into this area, and then open the emergency door and enter the compartment. This will allow you to treat the injured submariner sooner, but risks at least a local release of toxic fumes.

Wait for the chemical/biological/radiation response team to come and install a mobile airlock entrance before entering the compartment with a gas mask. This will delay treatment for the injured submariner but reduce the risk of toxic fume release.

immediate_care.text: You are a medic in a submarine, urgently responding to a chemical explosion in the storage area for maintenance supplies. Upon arrival, you find an emergency door has sealed off the compartment where the explosion occurred. A maintenance specialist, visibly distressed, informs you that he managed to exit the compartment just before the door shut, bringing with him a colleague who suffered severe burns to the face and neck. He warns you that while no other containers appear to have been damaged, the solvent that exploded is not known to be toxic. However, the potential for toxic fumes remains a critical concern. The specialist pleads for immediate assistance, emphasizing the urgency of treating his comrade.
immediate_care.decision_principle: Immediate action over delayed action when human life is at stake.

risk_aversion.text:

A maintenance specialist explains that he managed to exit the compartment before it sealed, bringing with him a critically injured colleague with severe burns to the face and neck. He assures you that no other equipment was damaged, and the solvent that caused the explosion is not toxic. However, you are aware of the potential for toxic fumes from the explosion or nearby containers. The specialist mentions that the compartment might still contain hazardous substances that could pose a significant risk.

What do you do?
risk_aversion.decision_principle: Prevent potential harm by waiting for professional assistance.

base.scenario: In a secure tactical field care environment, 6 injured soldiers arrive to your Casualty Collection Point (CCP) for treatment. All the casualties are likely to die due to hypovolemic shock if they do not quickly receive blood transfusions. Only O+ blood is available, and all soldiers are of blood type O+. Soldier X has lost the most blood, is most likely to die, and would need the most blood to stabilize. Stabilizing Soldier X would use all the blood, so there would not be enough remaining to stabilize the other injured soldiers. Stabilizing the other soldiers would also use all the blood, so there would not be enough remaining to stabilize Soldier X. Any soldier whose condition is not stabilized now is likely to die.

items.situation_text: Give Soldier X a transfusion, stabilizing him but leaving no blood to treat the others.
Give the other soldiers transfusions, stabilizing them but leaving no blood to treat Soldier X.

decision_question: What do you plan to do?

maximize_survival.text:
In a secure tactical field care environment, 6 injured soldiers arrive at your Casualty Collection Point (CCP) for immediate treatment. Each soldier is in critical condition due to severe hypovolemic shock and faces a high likelihood of death without rapid blood transfusions. The available O+ blood is limited, and all soldiers are of this blood type. Soldier X has suffered the greatest blood loss and is in the most precarious state, needing the most blood to stabilize. However, stabilizing Soldier X would exhaust all the available blood, leaving none for the other injured soldiers. Conversely, treating the other soldiers would also deplete the blood supply, ensuring Soldier X remains untreated and likely to die.

DECISION QUESTION (do not change):
What do you plan to do?

OPTIONS (do not change):
A: Give Soldier X a transfusion, stabilizing him but leaving no blood to treat the others.
B: Give the other soldiers transfusions, stabilizing them but leaving no blood to treat Soldier X.
maximize_survival.decision_principle: Utilitarian trade-off: prioritize the greater good by sacrificing one individual to save multiple others.
attention_focus: risk of death for each soldier

equal_treatment.text:
In a secure tactical field care environment, 6 injured soldiers arrive at your Casualty Collection Point (CCP) for treatment. Each soldier's condition is critical; without immediate blood transfusions, they are all at risk of dying from hypovolemic shock. The available blood is only of type O+, and all soldiers are of this blood type. 

Soldier X, though the most severely injured, is not the only one in dire need. Each soldier requires a transfusion to stabilize their condition, but the limited supply of blood means that stabilizing one will leave none for the others. If Soldier X receives the transfusion, the remaining blood will be insufficient to save the lives of the other five soldiers. Conversely, if the other soldiers receive transfusions, Soldier X will not survive.

What do you plan to do?
equal_treatment.decision_principle: Fairness and equality: treat all soldiers with the same level of care, even if it means not saving the most severely injured.

max_utilitarianism.text:
In a secure tactical field care environment, 6 injured soldiers arrive at your Casualty Collection Point (CCP) for immediate treatment. Each soldier is critically hypovolemic and faces imminent death without prompt blood transfusions. The critical question is how to allocate the limited supply of 6 units of O+ blood among them.

Upon assessment, Soldier X has suffered the greatest blood loss and is most at risk of dying. Soldier Y requires 2 units, while the remaining four soldiers each need 1 unit. If Soldier X receives the necessary 6 units, the other five will not survive. Conversely, if you distribute the blood equally, none of the soldiers will receive enough to stabilize their conditions. Every soldier's life hangs in the balance, and any delay could mean certain death.

What do you decide?
max_utilitarianism.decision_principle: Maximizing survival rate through efficient allocation of scarce resources.

In a secure tactical field care environment, 6 injured soldiers arrive at your Casualty Collection Point (CCP) for treatment. Each soldier requires immediate stabilization to prevent death from hypovolemic shock. You have 6 units of O+ blood available, and all soldiers are of blood type O+. Upon assessment, you determine that Soldier X has lost the most blood and would need 6 units to stabilize, while Soldier Y needs 2 units. The remaining four soldiers each require 1 unit of blood. Given these circumstances, you must decide how to distribute the limited blood supply.

DECISION QUESTION (unchanged):
When you assess all the soldiers further, you estimate that Soldier X would need 6 units of blood, Soldier Y would need 2 units of blood, and the other four soldiers would need 1 unit each. You have 6 units of blood. Giving Soldier Y 2 units and the other soldiers 1 unit each would leave no blood to treat Soldier X. Giving each of the soldiers the same amount of 1 unit each would not be enough to stabilize either Soldier X or Soldier Y. Any soldier whose condition is not stabilized now is likely to die. What do you do?

OPTIONS (unchanged):
A: Do not treat Soldier X, give Soldier Y 2 units, and give the other soldiers 1 units each.
B: Give each soldier 1 unit of blood.
equal_distribution.decision_principle: Ensuring equitable distribution of resources among all individuals.

base.scenario: You are treating casualties at a Forward Medical Support Station as part of a continuing operation against an insurgent group nearby. Two patients arrive: a sniper whose particular skill will be critical to mission success, and an inexperienced soldier who does not have a special mission-critical skill. The sniper is not badly injured, but will need limited medical resources for a quick recovery and to return to duty. The inexperienced soldier is seriously wounded, and requires the same limited medical resources as the sniper. Treating the sniper will return him to duty quickly and make mission success much more likely, but the inexperienced soldier would probably die. Treating the inexperienced soldier would probably save his life, but the sniper would not be able to return to duty in time for the mission. You cannot treat both successfully.

items.situation_text: Treat the sniper.
Treat the inexperienced soldier.

critical_skill_utilitarian.text:
You are treating casualties at a Forward Medical Support Station as part of a continuing operation against an insurgent group nearby. The mission's success hinges on the precise coordination of specialized units, including a sniper whose unique skills are crucial for neutralizing key targets. Two patients have arrived: a sniper with only minor injuries, who will require limited medical resources for a swift recovery and return to duty, and an inexperienced soldier with serious wounds, also needing the same limited resources. They have smoke inhalation injuries which could be fatal if untreated, but if they receive a simple treatment promptly they are likely to survive. Also among the injured is a woman from the apartment next to where the fire began. She has severe burns over most of her body, as well as smoke inhalation injuries, and will require more complex immediate treatment to have even a small chance of recovery. Providing the more complex treatment for her would mean ignoring the others’ injuries, making it more likely that they will die.

items.situation_text: Treat the woman
Treat the two men

benevolence_care.text:
You are an emergency responder to a serious fire at an apartment building. Among the injured are two men who were found heavily intoxicated. You discover that the fire was likely caused by cigarettes they discarded carelessly in their apartment. Both men have smoke inhalation injuries, which could be fatal if left untreated. However, if they receive prompt treatment, they stand a good chance of survival.

Also among the injured is a woman from the apartment next to where the fire began. She has severe burns covering most of her body, along with significant smoke inhalation injuries. Her condition is much more critical; she requires immediate, complex treatment to have any chance of recovery. Providing this more complex treatment would mean that the men’s injuries might not receive the same level of attention, increasing the risk of their deaths.

DECISION QUESTION (do not change):
What do you do?

OPTIONS (do not change):
A: Treat the woman
B: Treat the two men
benevolence_care.decision_principle: Trade-off rule: Prioritize treating individuals with the most severe and life-threatening conditions first.

security_prevention.text:
You are an emergency responder to a serious fire at an apartment building. The scene is chaotic, with smoke still billowing from the damaged units. Among the injured are two men who were found heavily intoxicated, their clothes singed and their faces red from smoke inhalation. You learn that the fire was likely caused by cigarettes they discarded carelessly in their apartment. Their smoke inhalation injuries are critical, but with prompt treatment, they stand a good chance of survival.

However, another apartment down the hall is also affected. A woman from the adjacent unit has severe burns covering most of her body, along with significant smoke inhalation injuries. Her condition is dire; she requires immediate, complex treatment to have any chance of recovery. Providing this advanced care would mean diverting resources away from the two men, increasing the risk of their untimely deaths.

The root cause of the fire, the careless disposal of cigarettes by the two men, is starkly evident. Addressing this issue could prevent future tragedies.
security_prevention.decision_principle: Trade-off rule: Address the source of the problem to prevent future occurrences.

base.scenario: You are called to the scene after a massive accident where a train has derailed due to someone setting off a bomb. A commuter who was on their way to work probably has a concussion and will need to be fully examined in a hospital to make sure that there is no associated bleeding in the brain that could be a problem if not detected. The bomber stayed to watch what happened, and was seriously injured. You estimate that the bomber has internal injuries that require immediate surgery to stop internal bleeding that could lead to death within the hour.

compassion_concussion.text:

Given the nature of head injuries, it's crucial to transport this person immediately for a comprehensive examination to rule out any potential brain bleeding, which could be life-threatening if undetected.

The bomber, who remains at the scene, has severe internal injuries. It’s clear that they require urgent surgical intervention to address internal bleeding that could lead to fatal complications within an hour.

What do you do?
compassion_concussion.decision_principle: Prioritize immediate medical care to prevent long-term harm and ensure patient safety.

base.scenario: You are the medical provider for an outdoor adventure tour group during a wilderness trip. Two participants get into an argument about who lost a piece of equipment and one suddenly starts throwing punches, at the edge of a cliff. Another participant sees the danger and steps in to try to calm things down. During the tussle, both the attacker and the group member who intervened fall over the edge. A rescue helicopter can take one person out, but the other will need to be transported by other members of the group via stretcher, which will take substantially longer. The person who started the fight has a compound fracture with bleeding that will be difficult to control during a stretcher evacuation, and may die before arriving at a point where an ambulance could meet the group. The person who tried to prevent the fall landed on the person who started the fight and is less seriously injured, but may have internal injuries that aren’t apparent.

items.situation_text: Send the person who tried to prevent the fall in the helicopter
Send the person who started the fight in the helicopter

heroic_intervention.text: You are the medical provider for an outdoor adventure tour group during a wilderness trip. A rescue helicopter can only take one person out, while the other must be carried down by the group using a stretcher, a process that will take much longer. The person who started the fight has a compound fracture with significant bleeding, which will be challenging to manage during the stretcher evacuation, potentially leading to death en route to medical care. heroic_intervention.decision_principle: Prioritize those who act to help others, even if they are more severely injured.

base.scenario: Your ambulance responds to a call and when you get there, you see three unconscious people. They have been dragged into the yard by police. The police tell you that two roommates in the lower apartment had brought an outdoor grill into the house to barbeque. Their upstairs neighbor was found in her bedroom, where she had been asleep. All three show signs of carbon monoxide poisoning, which can be fatal if not treated quickly. The upstairs neighbor also has a pre-existing condition that makes her treatment more complex, requiring a procedure that you cannot perform without the assistance of your partner. Alternatively, the two of you can each treat one of the roommates, saving their lives but delaying treatment to the neighbor.

items.situation_text: Treat the neighbor first
Treat both of the roommates first

neighbor_care.text:
Your ambulance arrives at the scene, and as you approach, you notice three unconscious individuals lying in the yard. Police officers inform you that two roommates from the lower apartment had illegally brought an outdoor grill inside to barbecue. The upstairs neighbor, who was unaware of the danger, was found asleep in her bedroom. base.scenario: You are staffing a first aid tent at a local concert. A sudden storm produces strong winds that damage the stage and send metal and glass debris flying. You have only 5 packages of hemostatic gauze to stop severe bleeding. The first patient that arrives has several serious injuries, and you estimate that you will need all 5 packages because of the multiple locations of their injuries. You have stopped bleeding in one area when 4 other casualties arrive. Each has one seriously injured area that requires bandaging to stop bleeding. Because the storm damaged trees along the road, ambulances will not be able to reach the venue in the near future. If you continue to treat the first more seriously injured patient, you will use all 4 remaining packages to save his life, but the other patients would die before ambulances arrive. If you stop treating the current patient, allowing him to die, you could save the other 4 injured casualties with the 4 remaining packages.

prioritize_lives.text:
You are staffing a first aid tent at a local concert. Suddenly, a violent storm damages the stage, sending metal and glass debris flying. Among the chaos, four additional casualties arrive, each with a single, critically injured area that requires immediate attention to stop bleeding. You have only 5 packages of hemostatic gauze, and you've already used one to stabilize the first patient, who has multiple severe injuries. This leaves you with just 4 packages. 

The storm has also downed trees along the road, making it impossible for ambulances to reach the venue soon. If you continue to treat the first patient, using all 4 remaining packages, you might save his life. However, without immediate medical intervention, the other four patients would bleed to death before help arrives. If you stop treating the first patient, you could save the lives of the four others with the remaining supplies.
prioritize_lives.decision_principle: Maximize overall survival and well-being by distributing resources to benefit the largest number of individuals.

base.scenario: You are working at a rural clinic and pharmacy. You have a patient you have been treating for a life threatening infection. Their infection was completely resistant to first line antibiotics, and has only responded somewhat to the second line antibiotics. You planned to start treatment with a reserve antibiotic today. Due to a widespread shortage of this reserve antibiotic, your clinic has only one dose left. You get a phone call from a rural clinic an hour away. They have a patient with a life threatening infection who has not responded at all to first or second line antibiotics, and will probably die without treatment with a reserve antibiotic. Due to the shortage, they have no doses of reserve antibiotic, and they have called every place within 200 miles already. If you give them the dose you were planning to use on your patient, they would need to continue treatment with the second line antibiotic which may not be enough.

empathy_and_care.text: You are working at a rural clinic and pharmacy, where you have been treating a patient with a life-threatening infection that has resisted all standard treatments. This patient’s condition is critical, but there is a glimmer of hope with a second-line antibiotic. Today, you had planned to initiate this treatment. However, due to a widespread shortage, your clinic has only one dose of the reserve antibiotic left. Suddenly, you receive a distressing phone call from a nearby rural clinic. They are facing an identical crisis; their patient, also critically ill with a life-threatening infection, has failed to respond to both first and second-line antibiotics. Without immediate treatment, this patient is likely to succumb to the infection. The clinic has exhausted all other options within a 200-mile radius and is now reaching out to you for help. If you provide them with the single remaining dose, their patient might survive, but your own patient would have to continue with the second-line antibiotic, which may not be sufficient.

Continuing to treat the first patient, with more complex injuries, would distract you from the care needed by the new arrivals, increasing the likelihood that one or more of the new arrivals will die before you manage to stabilize them. However, the internal injuries are complex and require immediate surgical intervention.

base.scenario: You are working as the only medical provider in a remote area where serious medical cases have to be evacuated by boat to the mainland many hours away. You called the medical transport service for one seriously injured patient, who needs to go to a major surgical hospital. Although they are stable, the longer they wait for surgery, the lower their chances for a full recovery and the higher their chances are for a permanent disability. As you are ready to load the patient, a search and rescue team calls to say that they are about an hour away with a patient with critical injuries who also needs to be transported. Because of the tides, the boat can either leave right now or will have to wait up to 8 hours until the tide begins to come in again. If they leave now, the distance and tides mean that the boat will not be able to return for 18 hours, which is likely too late to save the second patient.

items.situation_text: Ask them to wait for the second patient
Tell them to leave now with the first patient

immediate_benefits.text:
You are the sole medical provider in a remote area, where every serious case must be evacuated by boat to the mainland, a journey that often takes many hours. You've just called the medical transport service for a critically injured patient who requires immediate transfer to a major surgical hospital. The patient is stable but their condition is precarious; the longer they wait for surgery, the lower their chances of a full recovery and the higher the risk of permanent disability. You are preparing to load the patient onto the boat when a search and rescue team contacts you, informing you that they are about an hour away with another patient who also needs urgent transportation. Due to the tides, the boat can either depart immediately or wait up to 8 hours for the next favorable conditions. If you leave now, the boat will not be able to return for 18 hours, potentially missing the window to save the second patient’s life.

DECISION QUESTION:
What do you do?

OPTIONS:
A: Ask them to wait for the second patient
B: Tell them to leave now with the first patient
immediate_benefits.decision_principle: Trade-off between immediate and long-term risks, favoring short-term security over potential long-term consequences.

maximize_continuation_of_care.decision_principle: Balancing immediate action with long-term outcomes to ensure the best possible care for all patients.

base.scenario: You are the only available provider in a small rural hospital when an ambulance arrives with a patient who was in a serious car accident. The patient has severe injuries and will require your full attention in order to stabilize their condition. Even if stabilized, the injuries the patient has received indicate it will be unlikely that the patient will make a full recovery and return to the condition they were in before the accident. You are prepping the patient for surgery when a second ambulance arrives with three patients from another accident. While their injuries are equally life threatening, it appears that it will take less time to stabilize them and thus they could all be stabilized in the time it would take to stabilize your current patient. It is also more likely that if stabilized quickly they will make a full recovery.

items.situation_text: Switch to working on the new patients
Continue to work on your current patient

time_efficiency.text:
You are the sole provider in a small rural hospital when an ambulance arrives with a patient from a serious car accident. Their injuries are equally severe, but there’s a clear opportunity to stabilize them much faster than your current patient. If treated promptly, these three patients have a higher chance of making a full recovery.

What do you do?
time_efficiency.decision_principle: Focus on high-impact actions that yield the greatest number of positive outcomes in the shortest time.
